title: Protectionism & trade policy
summary:
## The big idea Unit 9 made the case for free trade; this chapter is the counter-argument — and the referee's verdict. **Protectionism** means restricting trade to shield domestic industries, and every government practises some of it. The exam wants three things from you: the **tariff diagram drawn perfectly**, a genuinely **balanced** for-and-against, and a verdict that respects both economics (mostly against) and politics (perpetually for). ## The instruments - **Tariffs** — taxes on imports: the import price rises, domestic producers expand, imports shrink, the government collects revenue — and society pays a hidden welfare bill (see the diagram). - **Quotas** — physical limits on import volumes. Price rises like a tariff, but the premium (**quota rent**) goes to whoever holds the import licences — often *foreign* exporters — not the treasury. - **Production subsidies** — pay domestic firms so they can undercut imports; the cost hides in the budget instead of the shop price. - **Non-tariff barriers** — safety standards, licensing, paperwork, deliberate port delays; **embargoes** ban trade outright. - **Exchange-rate manipulation** — hold the currency down to make exports artificially cheap (a recurring US–China accusation).
**What each part means:** under free trade the country buys at the **world price Pw**: domestic firms supply **Q1**, consumers buy **Q4**, and imports fill the gap Q1→Q4. A tariff lifts the price to **Pw+t**. Domestic supply expands to **Q2** (producers gain), consumption shrinks to **Q3**, and imports collapse to Q2→Q3. The **orange rectangle** (tariff × remaining imports) is **government revenue**. The two **red triangles** are pure **deadweight loss**: the left one is the **production distortion** (units now made at home at higher cost than the world price), the right one the **consumption distortion** (consumers priced out of units they valued above world cost). Consumers lose more than producers + government gain — that's the welfare verdict in one picture.
## The case FOR protection - **Infant industries** — new industries need shelter until they reach scale economies; South Korea protected its early car and steel industries. The catch: infants must eventually face the playground, and many never do. - **Declining ('sunset') industries** — slow the collapse to soften regional structural unemployment. - **Anti-dumping** — counter foreign goods sold below cost to destroy domestic rivals (legal under WTO rules when proven). - **Strategic industries** — food, energy, defence, semiconductors: efficiency isn't everything when supply chains are weapons. - **Employment and the current account** — politically potent, economically shakiest: protection saves visible jobs at invisible cost, and other countries retaliate. ## The case AGAINST - **Comparative advantage forgone** — higher prices, less choice, resources trapped in inefficient uses. - **Retaliation** — tariff wars shrink everyone's exports (the Smoot–Hawley lesson of the 1930s; the US–China tariffs of 2018–20 raised prices on both sides). - **Input costs rise** — a steel tariff taxes every carmaker; protection for one industry is a tax on others. - **Comfort breeds X-inefficiency** — sheltered firms stop improving. - **Regressive** — tariffs on food and clothing hit the poorest hardest. - **Capture** — concentrated producers lobby loudly; dispersed consumers pay quietly. Government failure in its purest form. ## Trading blocs and the WTO The **WTO** referees rules-based liberalisation and settles disputes (slowly — the Doha round stalled for decades). Meanwhile countries integrate regionally, in deepening stages: **free-trade area** (no internal tariffs) → **customs union** (+ common external tariff) → **common market** (+ free movement of labour and capital) → **economic/monetary union** (+ shared policies/currency — the EU). Blocs cut both ways: **trade creation** (members switch to a cheaper partner producer — efficiency gain) versus **trade diversion** (the common external tariff pushes members from the *cheapest world* producer to a dearer *member* — efficiency loss). Which dominates is an empirical question, bloc by bloc. **Globalisation** — ever-deeper integration of trade, capital, technology and people — has cut world poverty spectacularly while feeding anxieties over inequality, sovereignty and the environment; the 2020s' 'friend-shoring' and chip wars show the pendulum swinging back toward strategic protection. ## Why it matters Protectionism is the great synoptic battleground: comparative advantage, welfare analysis, market failure, employment, development and politics all collide. Perfect the tariff diagram — examiners ask for the revenue rectangle *and* both triangles — and always land a judgement: *narrow, temporary, rule-bound protection can be defended; broad, permanent protection rarely can.*
## ⚡ Quick revision - Tools: **tariffs** (price ↑, revenue to government), **quotas** (price ↑, rent to licence-holders), subsidies, non-tariff barriers, embargoes, weak-currency policy. - Tariff diagram: Pw → Pw+t; domestic output Q1→Q2; consumption Q4→Q3; imports shrink; **revenue rectangle + two deadweight triangles** (production + consumption distortions). - For: **infant industries**, sunset industries, **anti-dumping**, strategic security, jobs. Against: comparative advantage lost, **retaliation**, dearer inputs, X-inefficiency, regressive, lobbying capture. - Blocs: FTA → customs union → common market → economic/monetary union; **trade creation vs trade diversion**. - **WTO**: rules + dispute settlement. Judgement: selective & temporary maybe; general & permanent, no.

keyTerms.definition: Government restriction of trade to protect domestic industries from foreign competition.
A tax on imports that raises their price and reduces the quantity imported.
A physical limit on the quantity of a good that may be imported.
Non-tax restrictions such as standards, licensing, red tape or embargoes.
The case for protecting a new industry until it can compete internationally.
Selling exports below cost or below the home-market price to capture foreign markets.
Switching to lower-cost production within a trading bloc, raising efficiency.
Switching from a lower-cost non-member to a higher-cost member because of bloc tariffs.

diagrams: **Tariff diagram:** world price line, domestic supply and demand; add the tariff, then mark the rise in domestic output, fall in imports, government revenue rectangle, and the two deadweight-loss triangles.
**Quota diagram:** show the restricted import quantity raising price, with the quota rent going to importers rather than government.
Optional: a **trade-creation vs trade-diversion** sketch for a customs union.
tips: Draw the **tariff diagram fully**: revenue rectangle **and** the two welfare-loss triangles — a common high-mark requirement.
Give a **balanced case**: infant industry/employment/anti-dumping **for**, vs comparative advantage/retaliation/X-inefficiency **against**.
Contrast **tariffs (revenue to government)** with **quotas (rent to importers)**.
Distinguish **trade creation from trade diversion** when evaluating trading blocs.
Reach a **judgement** — protection may be justified temporarily/selectively (infant industry) but is usually welfare-reducing long term.

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Clear tariff/quota diagrams and protectionism debate.

keyConcepts.title: Trading blocs, trade creation and diversion

revisionNotes:
# Protectionism & trade policy — quick revision

**Instruments**
- **Tariff**: import tax → price ↑, domestic output ↑, imports ↓, government revenue, 2 DWL triangles.
- **Quota**: quantity limit → price ↑ but **rent goes to licence-holders**, not government.
- Subsidies (cost hidden in budget), non-tariff barriers (standards, red tape), embargoes, undervalued currency.

**The tariff diagram (drill it)**
- World price Pw → Pw+t; Q1→Q2 (domestic supply ↑), Q4→Q3 (consumption ↓), imports Q2–Q3.
- Revenue = t × imports (rectangle). DWL: production distortion (left triangle) + consumption distortion (right).
- Consumer loss > producer gain + revenue → net welfare loss.

**For protection** — infant industry (until scale; risk: never grows up), sunset industries (managed decline), anti-dumping, strategic security, jobs/current account (weakest — retaliation).

**Against** — comparative advantage forgone (prices ↑, choice ↓), retaliation/trade wars (Smoot–Hawley; US–China 2018), dearer inputs for other industries, X-inefficiency, regressive, lobby capture.

**Integration**
- FTA → customs union (common external tariff) → common market (factor mobility) → economic/monetary union (EU).
- **Trade creation** (cheaper member source) vs **trade diversion** (external tariff forces dearer member source).
- **WTO**: rules-based liberalisation + dispute settlement; Doha stagnation.

**Judgement template** — narrow, temporary, rule-bound protection defensible; broad and permanent welfare-reducing.
questions:
# Practice questions — Protectionism & trade policy **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** A key difference between a tariff and an equivalent import quota is that a tariff: - A — raises the domestic price while a quota lowers it - B — generates revenue for the government while a quota's premium goes to licence-holders - C — reduces imports while a quota does not - D — helps consumers while a quota helps producers
▸ Show answer

B. Both raise price and cut imports; the difference is who pockets the price gap — the treasury (tariff) or whoever holds import licences, often foreign exporters (quota rent).

**2.** On a tariff diagram, the deadweight welfare loss consists of: - A — the government revenue rectangle - B — the rise in producer surplus - C — the production distortion and consumption distortion triangles - D — the whole loss of consumer surplus
▸ Show answer

C. Some consumer-surplus loss transfers to producers and government; the two triangles are lost by everyone — high-cost home production and priced-out consumption.

**3.** The infant-industry argument holds that protection is justified when: - A — an industry is permanently uncompetitive - B — a new industry needs time to achieve economies of scale before facing world competition - C — foreign firms sell below cost - D — the country runs a trade surplus
▸ Show answer

B. Temporary shelter while scale/learning economies develop — with the standard caveat that protected infants often never grow up. C is the anti-dumping argument.

**4.** After joining a customs union, a country stops importing cheap wheat from a non-member and buys dearer wheat from a member country instead. This is an example of: - A — trade creation - B — trade diversion - C — dumping - D — comparative advantage
▸ Show answer

B. The common external tariff diverted purchases from the world's low-cost producer to a higher-cost member — an efficiency loss despite 'free trade' inside the bloc.

## Paper 4 style — data response > A middle-income country imposes a 25% tariff on imported steel to protect its mills, which employ 40,000 workers. Within two years: domestic steel output is up 15%; steel prices are 20% higher; the car and construction industries — employing 300,000 — report sharply higher costs and have shed 12,000 jobs; two trading partners have imposed retaliatory tariffs on the country's fruit exports; and the government has collected \$800 million in tariff revenue. **(a)** Define 'tariff'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, the effect of the tariff on the country's steel market. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse **two** unintended consequences of the tariff evident in the passage. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the tariff has served the country's economic interests. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A tax on imports, raising their domestic price. [1+1]

(b) Standard diagram: Pw → Pw+t; domestic output Q1→Q2 (the +15%), consumption falls, imports shrink; revenue rectangle (\$800m); two DWL triangles. Applied labels. [4]

(c) (i) Higher input costs downstream: steel-using industries (7.5× the employment) lose competitiveness — 12,000 jobs lost, likely exceeding steel jobs saved; (ii) retaliation: fruit exporters — bystanders — lose foreign markets. (Also creditable: consumers' higher prices, X-inefficiency risk.) [3+3]

(d) Gains: mill output/jobs, \$800m revenue. Costs: downstream job losses larger than steel employment gains, 20% dearer steel economy-wide, export retaliation, DWL. Judgement: concentrated visible gains vs dispersed larger losses — a net negative on this evidence; if steel is strategic, targeted subsidies would distort less. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain the methods a government can use to protect domestic industries from foreign competition. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the case for using protection to support an infant industry in a developing economy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Tariffs (diagram), quotas (rent point), subsidies, non-tariff barriers, embargoes, exchange-rate manipulation — mechanism for each.

(b) For: scale/learning economies need time (South Korea's steel and cars); capital-market failures stop private financing of the learning period. Against: governments pick winners badly; protection breeds complacency and lobbying (infants that never grow up — much of Latin American import substitution); consumers pay meanwhile; time-limits are hard to enforce. Judgement: valid in principle — conditional on credible sunset clauses, export discipline (the Korean test), and governance capacity.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, using the concepts of trade creation and trade diversion, how membership of a customs union affects an economy. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether the growth of regional trading blocs helps or harms the world economy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Definitions and mechanism of both; net effect depends on member cost structures vs the external tariff; example (EU agriculture diversion vs manufacturing creation).

(b) Helps: liberalisation achievable among willing partners when global rounds stall; scale, FDI magnetism, peace dividends. Harms: diversion, spaghetti-bowl rules of origin, blocs bargaining against each other, WTO undermined, outsiders (often poor countries) excluded. Judgement: 'building blocks vs stumbling blocks' — likely net positive with open external policies, but second-best to multilateral liberalisation.
